MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-30-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES: Atty. JENNIFER FIORE, ESQ. of Fiore Achermann (Tel: 415-550-0650) appearing for the plaintiffs;
Atty. PETER J. HIRSIG, ESQ. of McNamara Law Firm (Tel: 707-427-3998) appearing for defendant Aldon J. Smith;
Atty. RAZELLE ESPINOSA, ESQ. of Gilsleider, McMahon, Molinelli & Phan (Tel: 925-446-3117) appearing via CourtCall for defendant Shanice M. Hardy.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT ALDON J. SMITH'S MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Aldon J. Smith's motion to strike punitive damages in Plaintiffs Janice Frost and Sean Frost's complaint is denied. The allegations of negligent entrustment in the complaint are sufficient to support the punitive damages prayer. Because negligent entrustment is a negligence claim, there can be no finding of "malice" to support punitive damages without allegations showing intent to perform an act known to have a high probability of harm. (See Flores v. Enterprise Rent-A-Car Co. (2010) 188 Cal.App.4th 1055, 1063 [reversing denial of summary adjudication of negligent entrustment claim and punitive damages request where there was no evidence the defendant was impaired or otherwise unfit to drive when the vehicle was rented to him and statute circumscribed duty of rental car company to research DMV records of driver]; Truong v. Nguyen (2007) 156 Cal.App.4th 865, 875 ["Liability for negligent entrustment is determined by applying general principles of negligence"]; Taylor v. Superior Court (1979) 24 Cal.3d 890, 895-896 ["In order to justify an award of punitive damages on this basis, the plaintiff must establish that the defendant was aware of the probable dangerous consequences of his conduct, and that he wilfully and deliberately failed to avoid those consequences."].) Yet, while negligence, gross negligence, or even recklessness on their own are insufficient to form a basis for punitive damages under Civil Code section 3294, allegations showing conscious disregard of the rights and safety of others can give rise to punitive damages. (See G.D. Searle & Co. v. Superior Court (1975) 49 Cal.App.3d 22, 28-29, 32 [conscious disregard of safety may justify an exemplary damage award when nondeliberate injury is alleged].) Here, Plaintiffs allege that Defendant Smith knowingly allowed Defendant Hardy to drive a motor vehicle while she was under the influence of alcohol and impaired, and that he knew she could seriously injure or kill others as a result and that it was highly probable that harm to others could result. Knowledge of a high probability of harm emerges if the one who entrusts his vehicle knows the would-be driver is intoxicated. The principle announced in Taylor that willfully subjecting the public to the dangers of drunk driving activity can give rise to punitive damages may logically be extended to the person who hands the driver the keys while aware of the driver's impairment. (See Taylor, 24 Cal.3d at 897 [willful decision to combine "sharply impaired physical and mental faculties with a vehicle capable of great force and speed, reasonably may be held to exhibit a conscious disregard of the safety of others" regardless of whether "the driver had a prior history of drunk driving incidents"].) The degree of the vehicle owner's knowledge and the likelihood of the harm are factual issues better resolved at later stages of the proceedings after discovery.

Order signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: Sean Kane, Court Reporter: Anthony Vaughn, CSR #6185, E-MAIL: tonyvaughn@yahoo.com (302/EPS)

MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-30-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES: Atty. JENNIFER FIORE, ESQ. of Fiore Achermann (Tel: 415-550-0650) appearing for the plaintiffs;
Atty. PETER J. HIRSIG, ESQ. of McNamara Law Firm (Tel: 707-427-3998) appearing for defendant Aldon J. Smith;
Atty. RAZELLE ESPINOSA, ESQ. of Gilsleider, McMahon, Molinelli & Phan (Tel: 925-446-3117) appearing via CourtCall for defendant Shanice M. Hardy.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT SHANICE M. HARDY'S MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Shanice M. Hardy's motion to strike portions of Plaintiffs Janice Frost and Sean Frost's complaint is denied. The allegations that Defendant was driving unsafely while under the influence of alcohol are sufficient to support Plaintiffs' claim for punitive damages. (Taylor v. Superior Court (1979) 24 Cal.3d 890, 899 ["one who voluntarily commences, and thereafter continues, to consume alcoholic beverages to the point of intoxication, knowing from the outset that he must thereafter operate a motor vehicle demonstrates . . . 'such a conscious and deliberate disregard of the interests of others that his conduct may be called willful or wanton'"] [holding that trial court erred in sustaining demurrer to portion of complaint which sought recovery of punitive damages].) Defendant's attempt to distinguish Taylor and other similar cases on their facts is unpersuasive. Taylor held that while "aggravating factors" such as prior arrests and misconduct "may heighten the probability and foreseeability of an accident," they are not "essential prerequisites to the assessment of punitive damages in drunk driving cases." (Taylor, 24 Cal.3d at 896; see also id. at 897 ["One who willfully consumes alcoholic beverages to the point of intoxication, knowing that he thereafter must operate a motor vehicle, thereby combining sharply impaired physical and mental faculties with a vehicle capable of great force and speed, reasonably may be held to exhibit a conscious disregard of the safety of others. The effect may be lethal whether or not the driver had a prior history of drunk driving incidents"].) The essential gravamen of the complaint in Taylor was that "Defendant became intoxicated and thereafter drove a car while in that condition, despite his knowledge of the safety hazard he created thereby." (Id. at 896.) Plaintiff's allegations against Defendant are to the same effect. Dawes v. Superior Court (1980) 111 Cal.App.3d 82 reversed an order striking punitive damages allegations, concluding that allegations of running a stop sign, speeding, and zigzagging in a crowded area while intoxicated sufficiently stated a basis for punitive damages. (Id. at 88-89.) However, Dawes did not displace the threshold for pleading punitive damages in drunk driving cases established in Taylor; it merely concluded that there was no basis to strike allegations that went beyond the baseline set by Taylor. (See Dawes, 111 Cal.App.3d at 89-90.) Taylor was not overruled or disapproved following the 1987 amendments to Civil Code section 3294 that added the "despicable conduct" element to requests for punitive damages. (See College Hospital Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 725.) Taylor remains good law and compels this Court to find that Plaintiff's allegation of drunk driving is sufficient to withstand a motion to strike at the pleading stage. Whether there is sufficient evidence to ultimately support an award of punitive damages must be decided after Plaintiff has had a chance to conduct discovery.

Order signed in open court.
